coredata.dc:description: The treatment of chronic wounds remains a significant challenge in the clinical field, and optimizing the treatment plan through visual monitoring of wound healing is an effective way to solve such problem. Herein, we propose a feasible strategy to construct a smearable C–P-T/mCQDs hydrogel for real-time monitoring of wound infection and healing status, through the synergistic combination of modified carbon quantum dots (mCQD), cellulose nanofiber, tannic acid, and polyvinyl alcohol. The hydrogel can be readily applied on the skin and rapidly forms a gel dressing through high-density hydrogen bonding, demonstrating exceptional mechanical robustness (tensile elongation: 600 %) and autonomous self-healing capabilities. In particular, the carboxyl-rich mCQDs are more easily recognized by the sensitive pH-mediated polychromatic fluorescence response under ultraviolet excitation, exhibiting encouraging photodynamic therapy effect against bacterial infections. Under the irradiation of sunlight or near-infrared laser, the hydrogel achieves 99.99 % bactericidal efficacy against multiple types of bacteria (S. aureus, E. coli, P. aeruginosa, A. baumannii) within 20 min through reactive oxygen species generation. Furthermore, C–P-T/mCQDs demonstrates excellent antioxidant activity, biocompatibility, hemostatic efficiency and pro-healing properties. Notably, the mCQD-integrated hydrogel enables real-time, visual monitoring of wound status through its pH-responsive properties, providing substantial translational potential for personalized chronic wound management.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Morphology and structure of CQDs. (a) TEM and HRTEM images, (b) size distribution of particles, (c) Raman spectrum, (d) absorption (blue line), excitation (black line) and emission spectra (red line) (embedded: fluorescent physical image), (e) 3D-fluorescence spectra of CQDs. (f) Dehydration and condensation process in solvothermal reaction. (g) XPS spectra of CQDs, and the corresponding high-resolution C 1s, N 1s and O 1s XPS spectra. (h) FT-IR spectra, (i) 1H NMR spectrum, (j) Schematic diagram of preparation process and structure of mCQDs. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

Exploration of pH responsiveness. (a) Fluorescence luminescence (FL) intensity of CQDs at different pH and temperatures (excited at 365 nm). Reversibility of colorimetric response and FL intensity of CQDs at (b) 10–90 °C and (c) pH 1–13. (d) Physical images of reversible colorimetric response under different cycles. (e) Color comparison of CQDs and mCQDs solutions at different pH values under visible light and UV excitation. (f) Emission spectra of CQDs and mCQDs as pH increases from 0 to 14 (excited at 560 nm) and the locally magnified images. (g) Relationship between FL intensity and pH value of CQDs and mCQDs (excited at 365 nm and 560 nm). (h) The pH-response of mCQDs, and (i) density functional theory calculation results: the calculated obtained HOMO and LUMO, and the schematic diagram of bandgap variation. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

Morphology of hydrogel. (a) Photos of gel ink before and after 30 days of storage. (b) Physical images of smearing gel ink to the human skin and peeling off of hydrogel from the skin surface. (c) TEM images and size distribution of CNF. (d) FT-IR spectra of different hydrogels. (e) SEM micrographs of hydrogel samples and corresponding pore size distribution curves. (f) Correlation curves between pore size and porosity of different samples. (g) Strain sweep test (fixed at 1 rad/s) and (h) rheology curves of gel ink and C–P-T/mCQDs hydrogel (fixed at 200 % strain). (i) Rheological properties of C–P-T/mCQDs hydrogel.

Mechanical property of hydrogels. (a) Weight loss rate of different hydrogels. (b) Mechanism diagram of water-resistance of the C–P-T/mCQDs hydrogel. (c) Tensile stress-strain curves of different samples and photos of C–P-T/mCQDs hydrogel at different stretch lengths. (d) Cyclic tensile curve of the C–P-T/mCQDs hydrogel at a 50 % strain. (e) Tensile stress-strain curve under different strain rates. (f) Photos of adhesion of hydrogel to different materials and conformal adhesion to porcine skin and human skin. (g) Lap shear and (h) cyclically deformed test of adhesive force of C–P-T/mCQDs hydrogel on the porcine skin. (i) Schematics and optical photos related to the self-healing capability of the C–P-T/mCQDs hydrogel.

The pH responsiveness of C–P-T/mCQDs hydrogel. (a) Physical diagrams of hydrogel at different pH values under sunlight (Vis) and UV excitation, and corresponding RGB images (excited at 365 nm). FL intensity of the hydrogel at pH 4–9, excited at (b) 365 nm and (c) 560 nm, respectively. (d) Schematic diagram of pH sensitive hydrogel for monitoring chronic wound healing. (e) CIE chromatograms of the C–P-T/mCQDs hydrogel under different pH values. (f) Schematic and physical diagrams of using porcine skin to simulate actual monitoring of human wound healing under UV excitation, (g) FL intensity and (h) 3D-fluorescence spectra of the C–P-T/mCQDs hydrogel with pH reversible response ability after cyclic tests.

Antibacterial efficacies of different hydrogels. (a) Antibacterial rates against S. aureus, E. coli, P. aeruginosa and A. baumannii under different conditions. (Dark, 1 sun, and 2 sun equivalents correspond to 0, 100, and 200 mW/cm2, respectively) (b) Representative agar plate photographs and (c) antibacterial rates of the C–P-T/mCQDs hydrogel, and (d) the heatmap of p-values corresponding to (a). (e) Pictures of inhibition zones of different samples under different irradiation conditions. (f) SEM images of different living and dead bacteria before and after contact with C–P-T/mCQDs hydrogel. (g) Antibacterial rates of different samples against four types of bacteria under different irradiation times. Data are presented as mean with standard deviation as error bars. Statistical significance was calculated by one-way ANOVA. (∗p < 0.05, ∗∗p < 0.01, ∗∗∗p < 0.001, ∗∗∗∗p < 0.0001).

Explanation of antibacterial principle of hydrogel. (a) Growth curves of 106 CFU/mL S. aureus, E. coli, P. aeruginosa and A. baumannii after contact with different hydrogels. (b) In-situ radical-trapping EPR spectra of DMPO-•OH, TEMP-1O2, DMPO-•O2 − with the mCQDs, with and without light. (c) Photothermal properties of different hydrogels and their infrared thermogram irradiated with 200 mW/cm2 (d–e) Antibacterial ability of the C–P-T/mCQDs hydrogel against four kinds of bacteria after 15 min of 808 nm laser irradiation, and the infrared thermogram before and after 808 nm irradiation. (f) Schematic diagram of antibacterial mechanism of the C–P-T/mCQDs hydrogel.

Biocompatibility and promotion effect on wound healing. (a) Photos and (b) BCI ratios of blood clotting on different hydrogels. (c) Hemolysis ratio and optical images of hemolysis test on different hydrogels (n = 3). (d) Light microscope images of NIH-3T3 cells after 24/48 h of incubation with hydrogels. (e) In vivo schedule for different hydrogels to treat the wounds in mice. (f) Representative images and simulation analysis of skin wound healing by different treatments on days 0–15 (n = 3). (g) Wound closure appearance of mice treated by different hydrogels on Day 0–15 (n = 3), data are presented as mean with standard deviation as error bars. (h) H&E staining of the wound tissues in different groups (1: Control, 2: C–P/mCQDs, 3: C–P-T/mCQDs) on day 5 and 15, Scale bars = 400 μm, and corresponding enlarged local images (Scale bars = 60 μm). The yellow, blue, green, and red arrows represent adipocytes, fibrous tissue, inflammatory cells, and epithelialization of wound, respectively. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
Schematic illustration of the synthesis of (i) mCQDs and (ii) smearable hydrogel, and (iii) the application of sensitive pH-responsive hydrogel with PDT action for monitoring wound healing.

head.ce:title: Smearable CQD-entrapped hydrogel with sensitive pH response and photodynamic antibacterial properties for visual intelligent wound monitoring

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The treatment of chronic wounds remains a significant challenge in the clinical field, and optimizing the treatment plan through visual monitoring of wound healing is an effective way to solve such problem. Herein, we propose a feasible strategy to construct a smearable C–P-T/mCQDs hydrogel for real-time monitoring of wound infection and healing status, through the synergistic combination of modified carbon quantum dots (mCQD), cellulose nanofiber, tannic acid, and polyvinyl alcohol. The hydrogel can be readily applied on the skin and rapidly forms a gel dressing through high-density hydrogen bonding, demonstrating exceptional mechanical robustness (tensile elongation: 600 %) and autonomous self-healing capabilities. In particular, the carboxyl-rich mCQDs are more easily recognized by the sensitive pH-mediated polychromatic fluorescence response under ultraviolet excitation, exhibiting encouraging photodynamic therapy effect against bacterial infections. Under the irradiation of sunlight or near-infrared laser, the hydrogel achieves 99.99 % bactericidal efficacy against multiple types of bacteria (S. aureus, E. coli, P. aeruginosa, A. baumannii) within 20 min through reactive oxygen species generation. Furthermore, C–P-T/mCQDs demonstrates excellent antioxidant activity, biocompatibility, hemostatic efficiency and pro-healing properties. Notably, the mCQD-integrated hydrogel enables real-time, visual monitoring of wound status through its pH-responsive properties, providing substantial translational potential for personalized chronic wound management.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The healing of chronic wounds has been a major concern of clinical medicine for many years [1], especially for those with diabetes, mainly caused by ulcers, uncontrolled bacterial infection and many other factors [2]. In recent years, although great progress has been made in the treatment of diabetes wounds [3,4], challenges persist, including prolonged treatment durations and the inability to dynamically adjust therapies in accordance with real-time healing progression [5]. Recent research has underscored the pivotal role of pH monitoring in equipping clinicians with actionable insights into wound status [6]. This is because the pH of healthy skin typically falls within the range of 4–6 [7], whereas chronic wounds present a weakly alkaline microenvironment (pH 7.0–8.9), which not only facilitates bacterial proliferation but also compromises the healing process [8,9]. In view of this, the application of sensing technology provides a preferred solution to effectively real-time monitor the wound healing. The strategic integration of reactive oxygen species (ROS)-generating photodynamic nanomaterials with microenvironment-responsive systems significantly advances precision nanomedicine approaches for targeted infection control [10,11]. In recent years, gel materials have been widely used as wound excipients [12,13]. Although pH-responsive fluorescent hydrogels have been extensively investigated [14–16], their reliance on single-intensity signals often fails to resolve subtle pH variations critical for clinical decision-making. Therefore, there is an urgent need to develop a smart hydrogel with multi-color pH-mediated fluorescent response capability, with the expectation that it can accurately monitor the chronic wound and actively promote healing.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), as a new type of fluorescent nanomaterials, have been widely used in detection [17,18], fluorescent labeling [19], biological imaging [20], and even nanomedicine due to their unique optical properties [21,22]. In recent years, the synthesis techniques of CQDs have made great progress, and the photoluminescence properties of CQDs can be greatly improved by doping with heteroatoms [23,24]. Our previous research demonstrated that bright red light-emitting CQDs with high quantum yield (QY) can be obtained through the combination of nitrogen-sulfur co-doping and solvothermal treatment [25,26]. Some researchers also reported that reversible protonation and deprotonation of carboxyl groups on the CQD surface might induce pH-dependent fluorescence [27,28]. Specifically, the deprotonation of oxygen-containing functional groups, as well as the electrostatic doping/charging of CQDs and the shift of Fermi level, may lead to a redshift behavior from pH 1 to 13 [29]. Inspired by this, the development of CQD-based biomaterials with multi-color response shows potential application prospects in many fields.

Herein, we constructed a sensitive multi-color pH-responsive CQDs by grafting 1,2,3,4-butanetetracarboxylic acid, and further developed a smearable hydrogel composed of mCQDs, cellulose nanofibers (CNF), polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) and tannic acid (TA) (named C–P-T/mCQDs) through a simple one-step method (Scheme 1 ). The resulting C–P-T/mCQDs exhibits rapid sol-gel transition upon air exposure, forming a skin-adherent dressing through dense hydrogen-bonded network structures. Benefited from the introduced mCQDs, the wound infection and healing status can be real-time monitored by the sensitive pH-mediated polychromatic fluorescence response under ultraviolet excitation. Importantly, the composite C–P-T/mCQDs demonstrates potent photodynamic therapy (PDT) effect, achieving high bactericidal efficacy against multiple types of bacteria (S. aureus, E. coli, P. aeruginosa and A. baumanni) under the irradiation of sunlight or near-infrared laser (808 nm). Besides, in vivo evaluations of C–P-T/mCQDs using a murine full-thickness wound model revealed accelerated healing. The present work provides a feasible strategy for effectively monitoring of chronic wounds and promoting healing, and shows great potential for application on the clinical platforms of wound healing.
All the animals were free access to food and water. All animal experiments were approved by the Animal Ethics Committee of Jiangnan University (JN. No20240830b0601006[409]).

Citric acid (CA), thiourea (TU), 1-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)-3-ethyl-carbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC), N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS), 3,3′,5,5′-Tetramethylbenzidine (TMB), glutathione (GSH), and 5,5′-Dithiobis-(2-nitrobenzoic acid) (DTNB) were supplied by Shanghai Aladdin Reagent Co. (China). N, N-Dimethylformamide (DMF), ethyl alcohol, glycerol and 1,2,3,4-butane tetracarboxylic acid (BTCA), Nutrient Broth medium was supplied by Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (China). CNF suspension was purchased from Tianjin Woodelf Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (China). Polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) (alcohol solubility: 99 %) were provided by Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. (China). Rabbit blood anticoagulated with sodium citrate was purchased from Pingrui Biotechnology Beijing Co., Ltd (China). All chemicals were used as received without further purification. Clean grade BALB/c mice (6 weeks old, 20 ± 5 g) were purchased from GemPharmatech Co., Ltd (China). The Animal Ethics Committee of Jiangnan University approved all experiments involving animals.

Bright red-light-emitting CQDs were synthesized by a one-step solvothermal method. Firstly, 8 mmol of CA was diluted with 20 mL of DMF and then mixed with 40 mmol TU to form a clear solution. Subsequently, the mixture was transferred into a 100 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and heated at 160 °C and pH 1 for 6 h to realize carbonization and surface passivation. After cooling down to room temperature naturally, the obtained CQD dispersion was dialyzed to remove the residual precursors, then freeze-dried for later use.

To synthesize the mCQDs, 10 mL of phosphate buffer solution (pH 7.4) containing 0.25 g EDC and 0.15 g NHS was added to CQDs solutions (1 mg/mL, 10 mL), and the mixed solution was sonicated for 15 min in dark. Subsequently, 0.15 g BTCA was added to the mixed solutions, and then stirred at room temperature in the dark for 24 h to achieve the condensation reaction between the carboxyl groups in BTCA and the amino group on CQDs. Eventually, the mixture solution was dialyzed and freeze-dried for 48 h.

PVA (0.5 g), TA (0.3 g), and mCQDs (2 mg) were dissolved in 5 mL of mixed solvent (glycerol/water/2 wt% CNF/ethanol = 3:3:1:5, v/v), then stirred continuously at 90 °C for 2 h to obtain C–P-T/mCQDs gel ink. Generally, when the C–P-T/mCQDs gel ink was applied to the skin surface, an elastic hydrogel was spontaneously transformed after evaporation of solvent.

The determined results for each group were presented as mean ± standard deviations (SD), and the differences among groups were evaluated by one-way ANOVA followed by least significant difference (LSD). The probability p < 0.05 was considered as statistically significant. (∗p < 0.05, ∗∗p < 0.01, ∗∗∗p < 0.001, and ∗∗∗∗p < 0.0001).
More detailed experimental materials and methods can be found in the Supplementary Materials.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphology, structure and pH responsiveness of mCQDs
ce:section.ce:para:
The mCQDs with bright-red fluorescence emission were prepared by hydrothermal method using citric acid (CA) and thiourea (TU). Building on our previous finding that strongly acidic environments enhance red-light emission in CQDs [26], we systematically optimized reaction parameters to maximize fluorescence intensity. Crystal structure of the CQDs was analyzed by transmission electron microscopy (TEM), as shown in Fig. 1 a and b, the obtained nanodots are nearly spherical and uniformly dispersed with an average particle size of 3.56 nm. High-resolution images revealed distinct lattice fringes with a spacing of 0.24 nm, corresponding to the (002) crystallographic facet of graphitic carbon [30,31]. Raman spectrum of the CQDs exhibited characteristic D (1360 cm−1) and G (1640 cm−1) bands (Fig. 1c), confirming the coexistence of sp3-and sp2-hybridized carbon in the CQDs. Meanwhile, the high ratio of I
D
/I
G up to 0.9429 reveals a high graphitization degree of the CQDs [32]. Three significant absorption peaks at approximately 340, 466, and 555 nm were observed in the UV–vis absorption curves of CQDs (Fig. 1d), which are ascribed to the n-π∗ electron transition of the surface groups from CO, C–OH, CN and C–S [33]. In addition, according to the optical properties characterized by three-dimensional (3D) excitation-emission spectra (Fig. 1e), the maximum fluorescence emission wavelength of the prepared CQDs is at about 641 nm. Mechanistically, as shown in Fig. 1f, the precursors were successively condensed, dehydrated and carbonized in the solvent. That is, the precursors formed tiny spherical nuclei through aggregation after dehydration, and the remaining precursors further aggregated on the surface of the initially formed nucleus, ultimately formed CQDs.

Inspired by the positive contribution of carboxyl groups on the pH sensitive response of CQDs [34], modified CQDs (mCQDs) were prepared via grafting of BTCA onto CQD surface (Fig. 1j), aiming at expanding its potential applications in the biomedical field. The crystallinity of the CQDs before and after modification was analyzed by XRD, and the results are shown in Fig. S1. Element composition and surface functional group of the CQDs were also examined using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The full survey spectra in Fig. 1g show the characteristic peaks of C (284.08 eV), N (399.58 eV), O (532.08 eV) and S (164 eV), and the content of carbon is abundant, while the contents of N and S are the minimal. Comparatively, the contents of oxygen (31.26 %) and carbon (66.52 %) in mCQDs are significantly higher than those in CQDs, indicating the successfully introduction of BTCA on the CQD surface. The deconvoluted high-resolution C1s XPS spectrum indicates that mCQDs contain three components corresponding to CC/C–C (284.1 eV), C–O/C–N (285.6 eV), and CO/CN (287.6 eV), and the N 1s spectra confirms the consistent presence of nitrogen from pyridine (397.9 eV), pyrrolic (399.3 eV) and graphitic (400.1 eV). The high-resolution O 1s spectra can be deconvoluted into three peaks at approximately 530.4, 531.6 eV and 532.7 eV, representing –OH, C–O and CO groups, respectively. The above results implies that the sp2-carbon domains were functionalized with hydroxyl, carbonyl, ester, amino, and amide groups in the nanodots.

FT-IR spectra revealed the presence of characteristic functional groups in both CQDs and mCQDs, as shown in Fig. 1h, including broad –OH/-NH stretching vibrations (3000-3500 cm−1), –CO (1708 cm−1) and C–O/CS (1189 cm−1). The stretching vibrations of CC (1588 cm−1), CN (1624 cm−1) and C–N (1364 cm−1) suggest the formation of poly-aromatic structures in the frameworks [35]. Notably, mCQDs exhibited intensified peaks at 2881 cm−1, 1457 cm−1, 1346 cm−1, and 956 cm−1 (corresponding to the stretching vibration of OC–OH, CO, C–N/O–H, and C–O–C, respectively), mainly due to the introduced carboxyl groups of BTCA. The structure and composition of the CQDs were further characterized by 1H NMR. As depicted in Fig. 1i, two signals at 0.98 and 2.29 ppm were observed in the spectrum of mCQDs, which can be assigned to the hydrogen in the peptide bond and carboxyl. In addition, the signals at 7.63, 7.89 and 8.2 ppm are ascribed to the hydrogen in carboxybenzene, benzoyl group, and methoxycarbonyl on the benzene ring, respectively, demonstrating that BTCA was successfully coupled with one or more nanodots (Fig. 1j). Furthermore, the surface carboxylation of mCQDs, as confirmed by a zeta potential shift from −27.12 to −30.77 mV (Fig. S2a) and an increase C–O/CO ratio (Fig. 1g), not only enhanced colloidal stability and gram-positive selectivity but also synergistically interacted with BTCA-induced defect passivation to improve quantum yield (Fig. S2b).

To provide a comprehensive elucidation of the pH response mechanism, the response behavior of the prepared CQD at various pH levels was explored. The fluorescence characteristics under different pH and temperature conditions are presented in Fig. 2 a. With the increase of temperature, the fluorescence intensity exhibits a monotonic increase over a wide temperature range, showing a good linear relationship. This can be attributed to the thermal activation of surface defect states, as well as the thermally induced increase in non-radiative excitation recombination probability commonly observed in semiconductor quantum dots and core-shell structured quantum dots [29]. The fluorescence intensity of CQD can be restored regularly during cyclic heating and cooling in the range of 10–90 °C (Fig. 2b). Noticeably, as the number of cycles increased, the fluorescence intensity excited at 365 nm also gradually increased, which may be due to the loss of partially broken H-bonds during the cyclic heating and cooling. Overall, the synthesized CQDs demonstrated nearly perfect reversible responsiveness during acid-base cycling (Fig. 2c and d). This exceptional stability can be attributed to the pH-resistant carboxyl/quinone groups of the mCQDs, which provides feasibility for its application in biomedical fields.

The pH-dependent photoluminescence behavior of CQDs and mCQDs was systematically investigated to evaluate their sensing capabilities. Although pristine CQDs demonstrated broad pH responsiveness across pH 0–14 (Fig. 2e), their limited spectral differentiation within the physiologically relevant pH 4–9 range constrains practical utility. In Fig. S3, the peak positions in the absorption spectrum, attributed to the n-π∗ optical transition remain unaffected in the range of pH 0–9. As expected, the mCQDs have a sensitive more pH response in the pH range of 4–9 (Fig. 2f), which may be related to the functional groups such as carboxyl or amino groups on the mCQD surfaces. Moreover, it can be seen from Fig. 2g and Fig. S4 that the fluorescence intensity of CQDs increased with gradual increase of pH value from 0 to 10, while the mCQDs show more significant responsiveness in this range. The change in pH value may lead to changes in the electronic transitions of π-π∗ and n-π∗ in graphite nanodomains, by refilling or eliminating their valence bands [36]. By combining with OH−, a corresponding shell layer (protective shell on the surface) was formed on the surface of mCQD, as shown in Fig. 2h. As the pH increased, a redshift in the emission peak was observed, which may be due to the protonation and deprotonation of the surface functional groups in mCQDs, thereby leading to a shift in the Fermi level [27]. To some extent, mCQDs were isolated to reduce the rate of non-radiative recombination, ultimately exhibiting enhanced fluorescence. It is very interesting that pH 10 is the turning point for the photoluminescence of mCQDs, which undergoes a redshift in fluorescence under alkaline conditions. When the pH value exceeds 12, the photoluminescence intensity sharply decreases linearly within the pH range of 12–14, likely attributable to lactone-to-quinone structural transitions that disrupt conjugated π-systems.

To elucidate the pivotal role of BTCA modification in modulating the electronic structure of mCQDs, density functional theory (DFT) simulations were performed on both CQDs and mCQDs. The optimized structure, HOMO-LUMO bandgap, and corresponding structure are shown in Fig. 2i. The energy gap of CQDs is 1.51 eV, while the energy gap of mCQDs decreases to 1.24 eV. This may be due to the doping of BTCA, which connects the layers of mCQDs in the form of ester and peptide bonds, resulting in a decrease in the energy gap. Moreover, BTCA-induced surface oxidation reduces the LUMO energy through electron-withdrawing carboxyl groups, whereas extended π-conjugation elevates the HOMO energy level via defect state passivation. This electronic reconfiguration endows mCQDs with pH-responsive fluorescence switching (blue → red) and enhanced environmental sensitivity, outperforming conventional blue-emitting CQDs that lack tunable chromophores.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of mechanical property and pH responsiveness of the composite hydrogels
ce:section.ce:para: To address the critical need for real-time wound monitoring, we developed a multifunctional gel ink of C–P-T/mCQDs through systematic optimization of component ratios. This approach leverages the synergistic effects of CNF's structural reinforcement, PVA's flexible matrix, TA's robust bio-adhesion, and mCQDs' pH-responsive fluorescence. The precisely adjusted concentration of TA not only enhances interfacial adhesion but also stabilizes dynamic hydrogen-bonded networks. The gel ink has good fluidity and can maintain its original state even after 30 days of storage in a sealed state (Fig. 3 a). Meanwhile, as shown in Fig. 3b, it can be directly applied to the skin and then form a hydrogel layer through the rapid volatilization of ethanol, establishing a stable adhesion to the skin surface. The morphology and size of CNF, with 5.16 nm in average diameter and several micrometer lengths are shown in Fig. 3c. Meanwhile, the H-bond cross-linking in the hydrogels was characterized by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR). As shown in Fig. 3d, the wide absorption band at 3600-2980 cm−1 is mainly caused by the abundant O–H and N–H stretching vibrations on PVA, CNF, TA, and mCQDs. The samples containing TA exhibit new absorption peaks of CO and CC stretching vibrations at 1699 and 1614 cm−1, as well as stretching vibrations of C–H on the aromatic ring and bending deformation of C–OH at 761 cm−1.

Fig. 3e shows the surface morphologies of different freeze-dried hydrogels, including CNF/PVA (C–P), CNF/PVA/TA (C–P-T) and their mCQD-loaded hydrogel samples, revealing three-dimensional (3D) honeycomb-like porous architectures in all samples. The C–P hydrogel exhibited the largest pore size (3.77 μm) and highest porosity (87.29 %), whereas TA incorporation significantly reduced these parameters (pore size: 0.30 μm; porosity: 62.16 %). This reduction can be attributed to the abundant phenolic hydroxyl groups of TA, which form dense crosslinks with CNF and PVA via hydrogen bonding and π-π interactions. Furthermore, the abundant functional groups on mCQDs provide more hydrogen bond sites, making the network structure of C–P-T/mCQDs more compact (average pore size: 0.25 μm, porosity: 61.43 %). Moreover, the significant correlation between the average pore size and porosity can be obtained from Fig. 3f, validating that the increased crosslinking density directly governs microstructural consolidation.

As widely known, wound formation is always accompanied by the production of exudate. Generally, the hydrogel skeleton contains a large number of hydrophilic groups and porous structure [7], thus producing a certain swelling effect. However, excessive swelling may cause the hydrogel to fall off from the wound and even hinder the recovery process [16]. The swelling characteristics of the prepared hydrogel were examined, and the results in Fig. S5 show that the swelling ratio was significantly reduced after the addition of mCQDs and TA, which can be well matched with the porosity. Therefore, the highly interconnected porous network structure of C–P-T/mCQDs hydrogel promotes the capture of wound exudate, and provides suitable conditions for sensitive visual monitoring and promoting healing. Rheological characterization revealed superior mechanical stability in C–P-T/mCQDs, with storage modulus (G′) exceeding loss modulus (G") at strains <50 % (Fig. 3g), thereby confirming the elastic-dominant behavior critical for conformal wound coverage. When the strain exceeds 50 %, the intersection of G′ and G" curves indicates the collapse of the hydrogel sample. Meanwhile, the G′ and G'' of the C–P-T/mCQDs hydrogel are much larger than those of the ink-like hydrogel, indicating the better viscoelasticity of the formed hydrogel. To further investigate the differences in rheological behaviors between C–P-T/mCQDs hydrogel and corresponding gel ink, G′ and G" of each sample were measured under different frequency scans (Fig. 3h). For the gel ink, G′ is lower than G" over the entire frequency range, indicating that the ink shows fluid-like properties, while the hydrogel exhibits a gel-like elasticity (G' ≈ G"). Besides, the self-healing ability of C–P-T/mCQDs hydrogel was quantitatively evaluated by step oscillation (Fig. 3i). The hydrogel maintained structural integrity at a 1 % strain but exhibited flow behavior at a 200 % strain. Notably, the composite hydrogel gradually recovered its structural integrity upon strain removal, attributable to dynamic hydrogen bond reformation between TA's phenolic hydroxyl groups and the carboxyl/amine groups of mCQDs.

The water retention capacity of hydrogel dressings is critical for maintaining wound hydration and preventing exudate-induced maceration. As shown in Fig. 4 a, all hydrogels exhibited rapid initial mass loss within 20 min due to ethanol evaporation from the gel precursor, followed by stable mass retention over a period of more than 5 days, thereby confirming their robust long-term hydration preservation capability. Notably, the water retention of C–P-T/mCQDs is significantly superior to that of C–P/mCQDs, mainly because the free catechol group of tannic acid may allow for the formation of hydrogen bonding and Michael addition at the interface of hydrogel and skin (Fig. 4b) [37], which works together with sodium ion bond to provide reusable adhesion to the skin. This dual-adhesion strategy remained effective in aqueous environments, as TA and PVA molecules on the surface of hydrogel formed denser H-bonding networks to counteract swelling-induced adhesion loss. The mechanical properties of different hydrogels in Fig. 4c show that the introduction of TA and mCQDs is conducive to improving the tensile length of the hydrogel. Comparatively, C–P-T/mCQDs exhibits superior flexibility and tensile strength to P-T/mCQDs, proving that CNF plays an important role in improving the strength of hydrogels. The Young's modulus of C–P-T/mCQDs hydrogel reached 1.74 MPa at a maximum strain of 614 %, which is comparable to the modulus of soft skin tissue (0.1–2 MPa) [38], thereby showing good compatibility with the human skin. While C–P-T/mCQDs hydrogel maintaining time-dependent toughness enhancement (Fig. S6) through progressive H-bond reorganization under a 300 % strain over 6 days, which provides feasibility for long-term use. Also, C–P-T/mCQDs hydrogel exhibits good cyclic stability, and there is only slight deformation and strength reduction after 50 tensile cycles (Fig. 4d). Notably, the C–P-T/mCQDs hydrogel show good elasticity during cyclic tensile tests with different tensile strains (10 %–100 %), ensuring good adaptive wound monitoring of shape changes during exercise (Fig. 4e).

Remarkably, a 0.2-mm-thick C–P-T/mCQDs strip (7 × 2 cm) sustained 200-g loads without fracture (Fig. 4f), underscoring its structural robustness for clinical handling. The adhesive performance of the C–P-T/mCQDs hydrogel was rigorously evaluated across diverse substrates, demonstrating robust interfacial bonding critical for clinical applications. Fig. 4g–h and S7 show that the adhesion effects of the hydrogel to porcine skin can be well remained during shear and cyclic peel tests, providing the convenience for direct observation without additional dressing. The self-healing process is shown in Fig. 4i, it can be seen that the two pieces of cut hydrogels can rapidly self-heal, arising from the continuous reformation of hydrogen bonds between TA's phenolic hydroxyl groups and the carboxyl groups of mCQDs, allowing simultaneous restoration of both primary (elastic) and secondary (dissipative) network structures. Crucially, the self-healing of C–P-T/mCQDs not only restores the mechanical connection, but also restores its initial highly stretchable properties, ensuring long-term functional integrity in dynamic wound environments.

With the extensive research of hydrogels used for wound healing, it is particularly important to develop multi-color sensitive and responsive hydrogels that are easy to understand wound conditions. As illustrated in Fig. 5 a, the prepared C–P-T/mCQDs hydrogel shows a light color in the range of pH 4–9 under visible light, enabling clear visualization of underlying text and demonstrating high optical transparency. Additionally, it shows pH-dependent fluorescence shifts, with the corresponding chrominance data presented in Fig. S8. Furthermore, we converted the observed images into a remote RGB detecting mode via constructing a corresponding equation (pH vs RGB), so that the accurate pH value of the wound could be easily obtained in an intelligent approach. Interestingly, a slight difference in pH responsiveness between solution and hydrogel samples was detected, mainly owing to the influence of fluorescence emitted from TA, which is further proved by the fluorescence characteristics (Fig. S9). In addition, the stable fluorescence emitted by TA was verified by measuring the fluorescence emission of C–P-T hydrogel at different pH values (Fig. S10). Therefore, the sensitive pH responsiveness of C–P-T/mCQDs hydrogels is mainly contributed by mCQDs. From Fig. 5b, it can be clearly observed that fluorescence shifts towards green luminescence with the decrease of pH in acidic environments, while there is an increasing trend of blue luminescence with the increase of pH in alkaline environments. Fig. 5c demonstrates that mCQDs exhibit excellent red fluorescence emission in neutral environments, but show varying degrees of decrease with the change of pH values. Therefore, as shown in Fig. 5d, the hydrogel emitted blue fluorescence when bacterial infection occurred on the wound, and the fluorescence emission gradually changed to orange when the wound gradually healed to a normal state. The corresponding 3D-fluorescence spectra (Fig. S11) and CIE chromaticity coordinates (Fig. 5e) quantitatively map the full spectral response of the C–P-T/mCQDs hydrogel. Focused evaluations within the pH range of 6.5–9.0 (Fig. S12) confirm clinical-grade resolution, with a detectability of ΔpH = 0.5.

To validate clinical translatability, we simulated dynamic wound pH changes using a porcine skin model by subdermally injecting simulated tissue fluid (pH 4–9) to mimic infected and healing states (Fig. 5f). The C–P-T/mCQDs hydrogel exhibited distinct visual responsiveness across all pH conditions, thereby demonstrating real-time monitoring capabilities via colorimetric transitions aligned with exudate pH fluctuations. The recovery of wounds accompanied by constant changes in pH value is a dynamic process, which requires a reversible response of wound monitoring materials. In Fig. 5g–C-P-T/mCQDs hydrogel was immersed in acid-alkali tissue solutions successively for cyclic tests, the results reveal that the composite hydrogel has good reversibility, and the color response in Fig. 5h matches well with the results in Fig. 5a. Water contact angle (WCA) is a key indicator for evaluating hydrophobicity and surface wettability of hydrogel surface. As shown in Fig. S13 and a slight increase in WCA was observed with the decrease of pH value, mainly due to the protonation of abundant hydrophilic hydroxyl and carboxyl groups in C–P-T/mCQDs [39]. In addition, the porous structure of hydrogel can promote the infiltration of tissue fluid into the interior, thus enhancing the adsorption of liquid, which is conducive to rapidly monitoring pH changes in wounds.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antibacterial, cytocompatibility and hemostatic properties of the hydrogels
ce:section.ce:para: The gram-negative bacteria of Pseudomonas aeruginosa (P. aeruginosa) is a critical pathogen responsible for at least 10 % of acquired infections worldwide [40]. In addition, Acinetobacter baumannii (A. baumannii) is a nosocomial gram-negative pathogen with a robust outer membrane and the ability to acquire and retain extracellular DNA1, exhibiting multidrug resistance [41]. Here, we investigated the bacteriostatic ability of different hydrogels against Escherichia coli (E. coli, gram-negative bacteria), Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus, gram-positive bacteria), P. aeruginosa and A. baumannii. The antibacterial rates and the corresponding colony images on the agar plates are shown in Fig. 6a and Fig. S14-17, respectively. For the C–P-T/mCQDs hydrogel, it can be seen that the bactericidal rates against the four bacteria reached 99.99 % after incubation in the dark for 18 h (Fig. 6 b). Meanwhile, rapid bactericidal effect can be achieved after 20 min of irradiation (200 mW/cm2), with the bactericidal rates against S. aureus, P. aeruginosa and A. baumannii exceeding 94 % (Fig. 6c and d). Notably, C–P-T/mCQDs demonstrated superior performance in photodynamic bacterial elimination compared to C–P-T, mainly attributed to the enhanced ROS generation by mCQDs via defect-mediated charge separation. The slightly diminished efficacy against E. coli (80.5 %) is likely due to the intrinsic defenses of gram-negative bacteria, including their dual-membrane architecture, lipopolysaccharide-mediated permeability barriers and phosphatidylethanolamine-rich membranes, all of which collectively diminished ROS penetration and electrostatic engagement with the hydrogel's anionic matrix. Importantly, these photodynamic effects complement the inherent antibacterial activity of C–P-T/mCQDs hydrogel in the dark, thereby demonstrating dual-action antimicrobial efficacy for both immediate infection control and prolonged wound protection.

The disk-diffusion test was used to evaluate the antibacterial activity of different hydrogel samples, and the results are depicted in Fig. 6e and Fig. S18. Under dark conditions, the diameter of inhibition zone of C–P-T/mCQDs hydrogel was more than 16 mm, while that against P. aeruginosa reached as high as 27 mm, revealing a good release bactericidal effect. In contrast to C–P-T, the enhanced inhibition zone diameter can be attributed to synergistic interactions between polyphenols and photodynamic effects. Specifically, TA release disrupts bacterial membranes, whereas mCQD enhances the production of ROS locally. According to the results depicted in Fig. S19, the release antibacterial ability mainly depends on the entrapped TA. That is, TA contains abundant phenolic hydroxyl groups and can destroy the integrity of bacterial cell membranes by binding to bacterial cell walls [42,43]. As shown in Fig. 6f, the bacterial cell membranes in the control groups are smooth and flat, while the bacteria show morphological changes and deformation (the arrow indicates the site of membrane destruction) after co-culturing with C–P-T/mCQDs hydrogel, indicating that four kinds of bacteria were killed due to membrane destruction. Live/dead staining for S. aureus (Fig. S20) demonstrated that C–P-T/mCQDs induced membrane disruption (red PI) in contrast to C–P (green SYTO 9). This effect is attributed to the synergistic action of TA's polyphenol-lipid destabilization and mCQDs' ROS amplification, thereby collectively enabling dual-mode antimicrobial efficacy across diverse wound microenvironments. Besides, Fig. 6g show that the antibacterial ability of different hydrogel samples against four bacteria is positively correlated with the irradiation time of simulated sunlight (Fig. S21-24). When exposed to external simulated sunlight, the sunlight irradiation promoted the production of ROS by mCQDs to achieve antibacterial effects. The antibacterial rate of C–P-T/mCQDs hydrogel against most bacteria reached more than 80 % within 15 min, implying that C–P-T/mCQDs hydrogel has excellent PDT effect and is conducive to wound healing in medical treatment.

To evaluate the antibacterial efficiency of the hydrogels, 0.05 g of different samples were immersed in 1 mL of 106 CFU/mL E. coli, S. aureus, P. aeruginosa and A. baumannii suspensions, respectively. The bacterial suspension without any hydrogel was used as the positive control, and the Nutrient Broth medium was used as the negative control. The results in Fig. 7 a show that the C–P-T/mCQDs hydrogel can kill all bacteria within 12 h, showing excellent bactericidal effect. It is reported that CQDs have excellent photo-electric transfer properties and can generate ROS and heat by absorbing photons [44], which can efficiently destroy bacterial proteins, biomolecules and the outer membranes, leading to bacterial death [45]. To elucidate the mechanism of ROS generation by mCQDs under solar irradiation, electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) spectroscopy with spin-trapping agents was employed. As shown in Fig. 7b, three characteristic signals were detected under solar excitation, corresponding to DMPO-•O2 − (1:1:1:1), DMPO-•OH (1:2:2:1), and TEMP-1O2 (1:1:1), which contrasts sharply with the negligible signals observed under dark conditions. These results confirm the robust PDT efficacy of the C–P-T/mCQDs hydrogel, driven by multi-type ROS generation. Further investigation under 808 nm laser irradiation revealed a predominant production of 1O2, attributed to the wavelength-dependent activation of carboxyl groups on the surface of mCQDs and their energy transfer pathways. Collectively, these findings demonstrate the tunable ROS generation profile of the hydrogel, which can be strategically modulated by varying light sources to meet specific therapeutic requirements while maintaining potent antibacterial activity. Considering that the heat generated by PDT effect may cause some damages to the skin tissue, the temperature of the C–P-T/mCQDs hydrogel was recorded during 30 min of irradation (200 mW/cm2). The results in Fig. 7c show that the maximum temperature did not exceed 50 °C, suggesting its safety in the practical application of skin sticking therapy.

Currently, PDT using 808 nm laser has received widespread attention in antibacterial applications due to its advantages of temporal and spatial controllability, minimally invasive nature, effective disinfection, and avoidance of bacterial resistance [46]. We explored the bactericidal ability of the C–P-T/mCQDs hydrogel after 15 min of near-infrared irradiation (0.05 W/cm2), and the bactericidal rates all reached more than 98 % (Fig. 7d and e). Notably, the temperature of C–P-T/mCQDs hydrogel maintained a safe surface temperature of only 29 °C under 808 nm irradiation, eliminating any risk of thermal injury to wound tissue. Furthermore, the lower photothermal conversion efficiency (14.98 % compared to 19.59 % for CQDs) enhances PDT performance through BTCA-induced defects, which promote charge stabilization and significantly boost ROS generation while minimizing thermal effects (Fig. S25). To investigate the influence of hydrogel thickness on the efficacy of PDT, ROS capture experiments were conducted to evaluate permeability. The results demonstrated that the C–P-T/mCQD hydrogel exhibited a robust ability to induce ROS generation upon light exposure, thereby showcasing superior clinical PDT performance (Fig. S26). The corresponding photodynamic antibacterial mechanism is shown in Fig. 7f, in which mCQDs produce escaping electrons when excited by light. During the process of returning to the ground state, photon absorption accelerates the interband transition of electrons, resulting in fluorescence emission. At the same time, the electrons activated by light can transfer the π∗ orbitals of oxygen molecules to generate •O2 −, which can then be converted into several ROS. In addition, the excellent electronegativity and inherent surface functional groups of mCQDs prevent the electrostatic interactions with bacteria, and the addition of TA effectively captures bacteria (Fig. S27). With the generation of ROS on the surface of bacteria membrane, it gradually leads to the leakage of bacterial proteins, alters the permeability of bacterial membrane, eventually resulting in bacterial-specific inactivation [47]. Therefore, the C–P-T/mCQDs hydrogel exhibits excellent PDT ability in the visible near-infrared range.

Interestingly, TA is also reported to be beneficial for hemostasis, which is crucial for promoting wound healing [48]. As shown in Fig. 8 a, the C–P-T hydrogel demonstrated superior hemostatic efficacy compared to C–P (BCI: 46 % vs. 63 %), which can be attributed to TA entrapped in the matrix reacting with blood proteins (globulin, albumin) and coagulation factors. This reaction enhances fibrinogen adsorption and platelet activation. This hemostatic performance was further amplified in the C–P-T/mCQDs composite, achieving a BCI of 26 % within 10 min (Fig. 8b). The improvement can be attributed to synergistic mechanisms: specifically, the carboxyl groups of mCQDs (zeta potential: −30.77 mV) promote erythrocyte aggregation through electrostatic neutralization of cationic membrane proteins, while the polyphenolic motifs of TA enable covalent cross-linking with fibrinogen, thereby accelerating clot maturation via stabilized fibrin networks. The blood compatibility of the hydrogels was also evaluated by performing a hemolysis assay. As shown in Fig. 8c, the hemolysis ratios of all hydrogels are below 5 %, ensuring the safety of direct contact with wounds. Considering that excessive and uncontrolled ROS will lead to sustained oxidative stress and severely delay chronic wound repair [49], 1,1-diphenyl-2-picrylhydrazyl (DPPH) and 2,2′-Azinobis (3-ethylbenzothiazoline-6-sulfonic Acid Ammonium Salt) (ABTS) were used to quantitatively determine the radical scavenging rate of hydrogels (Fig. S28). The C–P control group showed the lowest clearance rate (less than 10 %), while the TA-containing hydrogel showed significantly higher clearance rates (98.5 % in DPPH, and 83.2 % in ABTS). This is due to the fact that the phenolic hydroxyl groups in TA can effectively inhibit the oxidation reactions by losing hydrogen atoms on the hydroxyl groups, forming free radicals and subsequently neutralizing other free radicals [50]. These results indicate that C–P-T/mCQDs hydrogel has high antioxidant capacity, which can protect the wound from oxidative damage and promote the wound healing. Meanwhile, the prepared C–P-T/mCQDs hydrogel has excellent biocompatibility (Fig. 8d), with a cell survival rate of 98.22 % (Fig. S29), and its controlled degradation behavior ensures the harmonious integration of therapeutic efficacy and systemic safety for clinical translation (Fig. S30).

To investigate the wound healing bioactivity of the C–P-T/mCQDs, we evaluated the ability of the composite hydrogel to promote skin regeneration by using a healthy mice skin defect model, so as to minimize the influence of inherent metabolic disorders or complications in diseased mice. Our experimental design deliberately excluded an independent C–P-T group (TA only) to prioritize the investigation of the core hypothesis regarding TA-mCQDs synergy. As shown in Fig. 8e, a full-thickness skin wound model with a diameter of 5 mm was established on the back skin of mice under aseptic conditions. The healing status of wounds subjected to various treatments was evaluated over a 15-day observation period. The representative images in Fig. 8f clearly show that both control and C–P/mCQDs groups revealed delayed wound healing processes, whereas the C–P-T/mCQDs group demonstrated a significantly increased wound closure rate throughout each time point (Fig. 8g). These findings confirm that TA enhances bacterial adhesion via its catechol moiety, thereby amplifying the local ROS generation by mCQD and inducing sustained oxidative stress on biofilms. Notably, the C–P-T/mCQDs group had a significantly higher wound closure rate (94.63 ± 2.1 %) on day 15 compared to the control group (87.09 ± 1.2 %), demonstrating excellent promotion effect of wound healing.

To comprehensively evaluate the therapeutic efficacy of hydrogels in wound repair, histological assessments via Hematoxylin and Eosin (H&E) staining were performed at critical healing phases (Fig. 8h). Consistent with established wound healing dynamics [51], the transition from pro-inflammatory M1 macrophages in early stages to regenerative M2 phenotypes orchestrates fibroblast activation and collagen deposition, which were significantly modulated by hydrogel composition. At day 5 post-treatment, all groups exhibited dense inflammatory cell infiltration. However, the control and C–P/mCQDs groups displayed substantial adipocyte accumulation, contrasting sharply with the C–P-T/mCQDs group, indicative of delayed resolution. On day 15, the control group remained trapped in chronic inflammation, as evidenced by persistent macrophage dominance, lack of epithelialization, and sparse capillary networks. In stark contrast, both the C–P/mCQDs and C–P-T/mCQDs groups achieved complete epithelial coverage with negligible inflammation response and absence of adipocyte invasion. Notably, the C–P-T/mCQDs hydrogel demonstrated superior regenerative performance, exhibiting robust angiogenesis and a well-organized, stratified tissue architecture with distinct epidermal, dermal and hypodermal layers that closely recapitulated native skin morphology. These findings corroborate the pivotal role of TA and mCQDs in synchronizing inflammation resolution with matrix remodeling, thereby underscoring the significant potential of the composite hydrogel we designed for promoting wound healing.

In summary, we developed a pH-sensitive smearable gel ink of C–P-T/mCQDs for monitoring wound infection and promoting wound healing, through the combination use of carboxylated CQDs, TA, CNF and PVA, which is easy to smear on the skin to form a flexible hydrogel layer suitable for wound of any shape. Once the wound is infected, the pH value of the wound micro-environment will change, causing the mCQD-loaded hydrogels to emit fluorescence in different colors under UV excitation, thus achieving a dynamic understanding of the wound status. Besides, the pH-sensitive hydrogel has excellent mechanical property, self-healing ability, antioxidant and bactericidal activities. Based on synergistic action of TA and mCQD-induced PDT effect, the C–P-T/mCQDs show high antibacterial effect against E. coli, S. aureus, P. aeruginosa and A. baumannii, accompanying by satisfactory biocompatibility. The investigation on skin wound healing in mice verified the excellent therapeutic effect of the C–P-T/mCQDs hydrogel. The present work provides a convenient strategy for constructing responsive antibacterial wound dressings, and has potential application prospects in the field of medical materials for wound monitoring and promoting healing.
